title: Metro de Madrid
summary: 12 lines, 300+ stations, the fastest way around the city. Open 6:00 AM to 1:30 AM.
content: Single ticket €1.50–2.00. 10-trip Metrobús €12.20 (works on metro + bus). Airport supplement €4.50. Free for kids under 4. Monthly unlimited pass €54.60. Apps: Metro de Madrid (official) and Moovit for real-time arrivals. Avoid rush hours (8–10 AM, 6–8 PM). Note: summer 2026 has planned closures on Lines 6, 7, 9B, and 10 for renovations — free shuttle buses cover affected sections.

title: Adolfo Suárez Madrid-Barajas Airport
summary: Spain's busiest airport, 12 km northeast of the city. 4 terminals + 1 satellite (T4S).
content: Metro Line 8 connects to the city centre (€4.50 supplement + €1.50 metro fare). Express bus 200/203 (€5, 40 min). Taxi €30–35 (30 min, can be longer in traffic). Renfe Cercanías commuter train from T4 to Atocha/Chamartín. Free shuttle between terminals.

title: Atocha Station
summary: Madrid's main train station. AVE high-speed to Barcelona (2h 30), Seville (2h 30), Valencia (1h 40).
content: Two parts: Atocha Cercanías (commuter trains) and Madrid Puerta de Atocha (long distance + AVE). Metro: Atocha Renfe (Line 1). Adjacent to the Reina Sofía museum. The original 19th-century hall is now a 4,000-plant tropical garden — worth a 10-minute wander even if you're not catching a train.

title: Chamartín Station
summary: Madrid's second major train station, north of centre. AVE to northwest Spain and Portugal.
content: Renovated 2022. Metro: Chamartín (Lines 1, 10). Buses to many northern destinations. Less crowded than Atocha.

title: EMT Buses
summary: City buses run by EMT Madrid. Same ticket as metro.
content: 200+ lines across the city. Single ride €1.50. Airport Express 200 and 203 to/from Barajas (€5). Night buses ('N' lines, 'búhos') run from 11:30 PM to 6:00 AM. EMT Madrid app has real-time arrivals. Same Metrobús 10-pack works on buses and metro.

title: Taxis in Madrid
summary: White with red diagonal stripe. Flag fall €1.70, €1.30/km day, €1.50/km night.
content: Available at stands (paradas) or hail from the street when the green 'libre' light is on. Supplements: airport €5.50, train stations €3. Ride-hailing apps FreeNow (formerly myTaxi), Cabify, and Uber all work. Always ask for a receipt ('recibo') if you leave something behind. Tip via app or round up cash.

title: Left Luggage (Consignas)
summary: Available at Atocha, Chamartín, and Barajas T2/T4. €4–6 per bag per day.
content: Lockers and staffed counters at all three. Atocha has the most options and is open longer. Useful for day trips where you want to drop bags after checkout or before check-in.

title: Renfe — National Trains
summary: Spain's national rail. AVE high-speed is the way to day-trip Toledo, Segovia, Barcelona.
content: Book on the Renfe app or renfe.com (in English). AVE tickets go on sale 9–11 months ahead; cheap 'Básico' fares often sell out. 'Elige' and 'Premium' fares include seat selection and changes. Day-trip from Madrid: Toledo (33 min), Segovia (35 min), Ávila (1h 10), Cuenca (1h), Córdoba (1h 45), Seville (2h 30).

title: Schengen Visa — US Citizens
summary: US citizens enter Spain visa-free for up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
content: Your passport must be valid for at least 3 months beyond your planned departure from the Schengen Area, issued within the last 10 years, and have at least 2 blank pages. Officially, you may be asked for proof of sufficient funds, return ticket, and travel insurance. The US has no ETIAS requirement yet in 2026 — it launches Q4 2026 with a grace period into 2027.

title: ETIAS — Coming Q4 2026
summary: New pre-travel authorisation for visa-exempt visitors to the Schengen Area.
content: ETIAS launches in the last quarter of 2026 (likely October/November) with a transitional grace period of approximately 6 months running into April 2027. Cost: €20 per application (free for under-18 and over-70). Validity: 3 years. Required for US, UK, Canadian, Australian, and most other visa-exempt nationals. Apply online at etias.eu — most approvals take minutes.

title: Schengen Visa — UK Citizens
summary: UK citizens enter Spain visa-free for up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
content: Same 90/180-day rule as other visa-exempt nationals. Passport must be valid for at least 3 months beyond departure, issued within the last 10 years, and have 2 blank pages. ETIAS will also apply to UK citizens from Q4 2026.

title: Passport Requirements
summary: 3 months validity beyond departure, issued within last 10 years, 2 blank pages.
content: These are the Schengen-standard rules applied at Spanish border control. If your passport is close to expiry or was issued more than 10 years ago, renew before you travel — the airline may also refuse boarding.

title: Euros & ATMs
summary: Spain uses the Euro. ATMs are everywhere; avoid the Euronet ATMs in tourist traps.
content: Withdraw from bank-affiliated ATMs (CaixaBank, BBVA, Santander, Bankinter) for the best rates. Euronet ATMs in tourist areas add heavy fees. Most Spanish banks charge a small fee for non-customer withdrawals — check with your home bank about foreign transaction fees.

title: Credit Cards & Contactless
summary: Visa and Mastercard widely accepted. American Express hit-or-miss outside hotels.
content: Spain is very card-friendly in cities. Smaller bars, taxis, and market stalls may prefer cash or have a minimum for cards (typically €5–10). Contactless ('contactless sin contacto') works almost everywhere. Notify your bank of travel to avoid fraud blocks.

title: Tipping Etiquette
summary: Not required, but appreciated. Round up or leave 5–10% for good service.
content: Service is included by law. Tipping is genuinely optional. Common practice: leave small change from a coffee (€0.20–0.50), 5–10% in sit-down restaurants if service was great, €1 per bag for hotel porters, round up taxi fares. Don't tip at bars counter service.

title: Daily Budgets
summary: Budget €60–100/day mid-range, €150+ for a nicer trip, €40 backpacker-style.
content: Mid-range: €100–150 hotel, €25–40 food, €15 transport, €20 attractions = ~€160–225/day. Backpacker: hostel €25–40, supermarket breakfast €5, menu del día €12, public transport €10 = ~€60–80/day. Luxury: €300+ hotels, fine dining €100+ per person.

title: Survival Spanish — 20 Phrases
summary: Hola, gracias, por favor, ¿dónde está…?, la cuenta, por favor. The basics that get you everywhere.
content: Top 20: ¡Hola! / ¿Qué tal? / Buenos días / Buenas tardes / Adiós / Por favor / Gracias / De nada / Sí / No / Perdón / Lo siento / No entiendo / ¿Habla inglés? / ¿Dónde está…? / ¿Cuánto cuesta? / La cuenta, por favor / Una mesa para dos / Con permiso / ¡Salud! (cheers). Spaniards are forgiving and delighted when foreigners try — even a 'buenos días' wins you points.

title: Emergency Numbers
summary: 112 is the universal EU emergency line. Police: 091. Ambulance: 061.
content: 112 works in English, French, German, and Spanish. Free from any phone. Police (Policía Nacional) 091. Local police (Policía Municipal) 092. Ambulance (SUMMA) 061. Fire 080. Civil Guard 062. For non-urgent tourist help, contact the Tourist Attention Service on +34 91 522 00 81.

title: Hospitals & 24h Clinics
summary: Hospital La Paz, Hospital Gregorio Marañón, Hospital Ramón y Cajal — all public, all with English-speaking staff.
content: Public hospitals (hospitales públicos) provide emergency care to anyone. For non-emergencies, private hospitals (HM Hospitales, Quirónsalud, Sanitas) are faster and many have English-speaking coordinators. EU citizens: use your EHIC/GHIC card for public care. Non-EU: pay upfront or use travel insurance.

title: Embassies & Consulates
summary: Your embassy is the right call for lost/stolen passports, arrests, and serious emergencies.
content: US Embassy: Calle de Serrano, 75. UK Consulate: Torre Emperador, Paseo de la Castellana. Both take appointments for non-emergency consular services. For a lost passport, file a police report (denuncia) first online or at any police station.

title: Lost or Stolen Passport
summary: File a police report (denuncia) immediately, then contact your embassy for an emergency travel document.
content: Online police reports at denuncias.policia.es (works for non-residents). Take the report and your embassy emergency travel document to a Spanish police station for exit clearance. Carry photocopies of your passport separately while travelling.

title: Pharmacies (Farmacias)
summary: Green cross signs. Most open 9:30 AM to 9:30 PM; rota pharmacy covers every neighbourhood 24h.
content: Look for the green cross. Every district has a 'farmacia de guardia' open 24h — schedules are posted on every pharmacy door. Pharmacists can give advice and sell most non-prescription medications that need a prescription elsewhere. For serious issues, go to a hospital.

title: SIM Cards & eSIMs
summary: Buy a Spanish SIM or eSIM on arrival. Major carriers: Movistar, Orange, Vodafone, Yoigo.
content: Free tourist SIMs (15–30 days) available at the airport and carrier stores — bring your passport. eSIMs from Airalo, Holafly, and Nomad work before you fly. All major carriers have English apps. Cheapest data-only: €5–10/10GB. Note: Spain does NOT require tourist ID registration for SIMs as of 2026.

title: Public Wi-Fi & Coworking
summary: Free Wi-Fi at airport, metro stations, libraries, and most cafés. Coworking spaces across the city.
content: Free Wi-Fi: AENA airports, Metro Madrid stations, bibliotecas (libraries), major café chains. Coworking: WeWork, Impact Hub, The Valley, Utopicus, Monday — day passes €20–35, monthly €150–300. Most cafés are laptop-friendly mid-morning and mid-afternoon; avoid lunch rush.

title: NIE / TIE — Foreigner ID
summary: NIE is the foreigner ID number; TIE is the physical card. Required for everything official.
content: NIE (Número de Identidad de Extranjero): apply at any Oficina de Extranjería with passport, EX-15 form, reason for needing it (job, studies, property), €10.80 fee. TIE (Tarjeta de Identidad de Extranjero): physical card for non-EU residents with a permit of 6+ months. Apply at the police station (Comisaría) on Calle Silva or Montera after your residence permit is approved. Both require appointments — book weeks ahead.

title: Empadronamiento
summary: Register at your local town hall. Unlocks healthcare, school enrolment, and many other services.
content: Go to your district's Junta Municipal with passport, NIE (if you have one), and proof of address (rental contract or 'autorización del propietario' + utility bill). Free, same-day, child-friendly. Renew every 2 years (you'll get a postcard). Without the padron you can't register for public healthcare or enrol kids in school.

title: Idealista — Apartment Search
summary: Spain's #1 property portal. Used by everyone from students to expats.
content: Available in English and Spanish. Filter by neighborhood, price, size, furnished, pets allowed. Most listings are by agencies — expect 'one month deposit + one month guarantee' (fianza + aval) and agency fees of 1 month's rent. Use the map view to compare locations. Watch out for scams: never transfer money before viewing.

title: Fotocasa — Property Portal
summary: Major alternative to Idealista, with a similar volume of listings.
content: Same basic use cases as Idealista. Both portals list overlapping inventory. Worth checking both when apartment hunting.

title: Madrid Neighborhoods Guide
summary: Choose your barrio based on vibe: Malasaña for indie, Salamanca for upscale, Chamberí for families, Chueca for LGBT+, Lavapiés for gritty, Retiro for calm.
content: Centro: tourist-heavy, walkable, lively. Malasaña: indie shops, bars, students. Chueca: design shops, restaurants, LGBT+. Chamberí: families, traditional, calmer. Salamanca: luxury shopping, business. Retiro: parks, family-friendly. Lavapiés: multicultural, edgy, affordable. La Latina: old-town charm, tapas. Arganzuela: up-and-coming, near Madrid Río.

title: Rental Contracts & Deposits
summary: Standard deposit: 1 month. Some landlords want 2 months + 1 month 'aval' bank guarantee.
content: Standard is '1 mes de fianza' (1 month deposit) returned within 30 days of moving out. Avoid 'aval bancario' if possible — it's a bank guarantee that ties up your money. Contract length typically 11 months (avoids tourist-license complications for landlord). Always get a written inventory ('inventario').

title: Spanish Bank Accounts
summary: Big 5: CaixaBank, BBVA, Santander, Bankinter, Sabadell. Most expat-friendly: Wise, N26, Revolut.
content: To open a Spanish account you'll need: NIE, empadronamiento, employment contract or 'nómina', and sometimes a minimum deposit. Online banks like Wise and N26 work for non-residents and are accepted almost everywhere. Avoid the touristy branches in Centro — appointments take weeks; use suburban branches or app-based banks.

title: Public Healthcare (Seguridad Social)
summary: Free or low-cost healthcare for residents. Get a 'tarjeta sanitaria' to access GPs and hospitals.
content: After registering on the padron and starting work (or as a 'convenio especial' for €60/month if self-funded), go to your local Centro de Salud with NIE + padron certificate + employment proof to get a tarjeta sanitaria. Assigns you a GP. Emergency care is universal — anyone can walk into a public hospital ER.

title: English-Speaking Doctors
summary: Madrid has many English-speaking GPs and specialists, mostly in the private system.
content: Madrid Healthcare (Centro Médico), Hospital HM Sanchinarro, Centro de Salud Internacional — all have English-speaking staff. Speakeasy Madrid, the American Health Group, and the British-American Clinic are all known to expats. For emergencies, the public system is fine — many doctors speak some English.

title: Teach English in Spain
summary: Auxiliares de Conversación is the official program; private academies hire year-round.
content: Auxiliares de Conversación: government program placing native English speakers in Spanish schools. €1,000–1,200/month, ~16 hrs/week. Apply January–March for September start. Private academies (British Council, International House, ELA, Británico) hire year-round — TEFL required, €1,200–1,800/month. Get a NIE before starting.

title: Au Pair in Spain
summary: Live with a Spanish family, help with childcare, get pocket money + room & board.
content: Agencies: Cultural Care Au Pair, AuPairWorld, GreatAuPair. Typical: €80–120/week, own room, 1–2 days off, language course included. Visa is informal for EU citizens; non-EU need a 'visado de au pair' which is a specific work permit.

title: Madrid Universities
summary: Universidad Complutense (UCM) is Spain's largest. Also: UAM, UPM, UAH, URJC, IE, ESADE, ESERP.
content: Public universities (UCM, UAM, UPM) are €1,000–2,500/year for EU students, cheaper than most of Europe. Private business schools (IE, ESADE, IESE, ESERP) charge €20,000–40,000/year but offer international MBAs in English. Admissions through UNED for most public programs.

title: Spanish Language Schools
summary: Tandem Madrid, Don Quijote, Estudio Sampere, AIL Madrid, Españolé.
content: €150–350/week for 20 hrs/week group classes. Intensive courses €1,000–2,000/month. Most offer homestay or shared flat accommodation. Cervantes Institute accreditation is the gold standard. Book 2–4 weeks ahead in summer.

title: Escuela Oficial de Idiomas (EOI)
summary: Official government language school. Cheap Spanish classes — €100–300/year.
content: EOIs teach Spanish as a foreign language (ELE), English, French, German, and more. Subsidised by the Comunidad de Madrid. Admission is by 'prueba de nivelación' (level test) in September. Free or nearly free. Centro: Jesús Maestro (Islas Filipinas metro), Aluche, Getafe, Móstoles.

title: Museo del Prado
summary: Spain's #1 museum. Velázquez, Goya, El Greco, Bosch, Rubens, Titian. Free Mon–Sat 6–8 PM and Sundays.
content: Over 8,000 paintings. Plan at least 3 hours. The Velázquez, Goya, and El Greco rooms are unmissable. Avoid the free evening hours if you don't like crowds. Skip-the-line tickets €15 general, €7 reduced. Free for under-18 and over-65. Audio guide €5, well worth it.

title: Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía
summary: Spain's modern art museum. Picasso's Guernica, Dalí, Miró. Free Mon, Wed–Sat 7–9 PM, Sun 12:30–2:30 PM.
content: Guernica is on the 2nd floor of the Sabatini Building — go straight there if you have limited time. €12 general, free for under-18 and over-65. Combined ticket with the Prado: €22 (no time limit). The Reina Sofía and Atocha station are 5 min apart — combine with the Thyssen (a 10-min walk) for the Golden Triangle in a day.

title: Museo Nacional Thyssen-Bornemisza
summary: The third leg of the Golden Triangle. Italian, Dutch, German, and 20th-century masters. €15 general.
content: Caravaggio, Van Eyck, Dürer, Monet, Hopper, Picasso, Pollock. The collection reads like a tour of Western painting from the 13th to 20th century. €15 general, free on Mondays 12–4 PM. Permanent collection is well laid out chronologically — easier to navigate than the Prado.

title: Royal Palace (Palacio Real)
summary: Spain's official royal residence (the King lives at Zarzuela). 3,418 rooms, 3rd-largest palace in Europe.
content: €12 entry, free for EU citizens Mon–Thu 4–6 PM (off-season) — bring passport. Closed for state ceremonies. Highlights: Throne Room, Hall of Mirrors (modeled on Versailles), Royal Chapel, Royal Armoury. Plan 1.5–2 hours. Combined ticket with the Almudena Cathedral €16.

title: Catedral de la Almudena
summary: Madrid's cathedral, finished in 1993 after 400 years of construction. Faces the Royal Palace.
content: Free to enter; museum and dome €7. Mix of neoclassical, neo-Gothic, and modern. The crypt is striking. The dome offers 360° views of central Madrid. Mass held daily. Same architect (Fernando Chueca Goitia) as the rest of post-civil-war Madrid.

title: Parque del Retiro
summary: 350-acre park in the heart of the city. Lake, Crystal Palace, rose garden, puppet shows.
content: Free, open daily 6 AM to midnight. The Estanque (lake) has rowboats €6. Crystal Palace hosts temporary art exhibitions. Sundays around the Palacio de Cristal are full of street performers and families. Watch your pockets around the south end (close to Atocha).

title: Casa de Campo
summary: 1,700 hectares — 5x larger than Hyde Park. Zoo, amusement park, lake, hiking.
content: Metro: Lago, Batán. Free entry. The Parque de Atracciones (amusement park) and Zoo are paid but adjacent. The lake has paddle boats. Great for jogging and cycling on weekdays when it's quiet. Avoid the underpasses at night.

title: Madrid Río
summary: 6 km of riverside park along the Manzanares. Bike paths, playgrounds, public art.
content: Reclaimed riverbank from a buried motorway. Runs from Puente de los Franceses to Puente de Arganzuela. Connected to Casa de Campo by the Puente de Toledo. Beaches and wading pools in summer. Cycling is the best way to see it all in one go.

title: Teleférico (Cable Car)
summary: Cable car from the Rosaleda in Retiro Park to Casa de Campo. 11 min, panoramic views.
content: €6.50 one-way, €9.20 round-trip. Operates 11 AM to dusk (closed in high winds). Not the most dramatic cable car in the world but a pleasant ride and a fun way to connect the two parks. Combine with Retiro and a picnic in Casa de Campo.

title: Plaza Mayor
summary: 17th-century arcaded square in the heart of Habsburg Madrid. Now mostly tourist cafés.
content: Built 1619, the social centre of imperial Madrid. The Casa de la Panadería (bakery) is the pink building on the north side. Christmas market and New Year's Eve grape-eating tradition take place here. The arcaded walkways shelter souvenir shops and overpriced restaurants — eat just outside the square instead.

title: Gran Vía
summary: Madrid's main shopping and theatre street. 'The Spanish Broadway.' Built 1910.
content: 1.3 km from Calle de Alcalá to Plaza de España. Flagship stores (Zara, Primark, H&M), theatres (Lope de Vega, Rialto), cinemas, and the iconic Capitol building with its Schweppes sign. Best walked at sunset when the neon comes on. Lots of rooftop bars on the upper floors of the older buildings.

title: Puerta del Sol
summary: The kilometre-zero plaza. The Bear and the Strawberry Tree statue. New Year's bell.
content: Where Spain's kilometre-zero road marker sits. The 20-tonne bell at Casa de Correos marks midnight on New Year's Eve — eat 12 grapes, one for each chime. Often crowded; best at sunrise or late evening. Surrounding streets are packed with the most expensive retail rents in Spain.

title: Templo de Debod
summary: A 2,200-year-old Egyptian temple, gifted to Spain in 1968 for saving the Abu Simbel temples.
content: Free entry. In the Parque del Oeste, near Príncipe Pío. Open Tue–Sun 10 AM to 8 PM (summer) / 6 PM (winter). Sunset from the temple grounds is one of Madrid's best-kept secrets. The walk up through the park is lovely; couple it with the Cable Car from the Rosaleda.

title: Toledo — Day Trip
summary: UNESCO old town, 30 min by AVE. The El Greco, the cathedral, the synagogue quarter, swords.
content: AVE from Atocha: 33 min. Bus from Plaza Elíptica: 50 min. The old town is a single hilltop, best walked. Toledo is famous for steelwork and sword-making — the Visigothic-era techniques are still used. The cathedral is one of Spain's finest Gothic buildings. Try the local 'carne de caza' (game meat) and 'mazapán' (marzipan).

title: Segovia — Day Trip
summary: Roman aqueduct, Alcázar castle, cathedral. 35 min by AVE.
content: AVE from Chamartín: 28 min. The Roman aqueduct (Acueducto) is 2,000 years old, 28m tall, and has 167 arches — still intact. The Alcázar (fairy-tale castle) is said to have inspired Disney. Famous for 'cochinillo asado' (roast suckling pig) — Mesón de Cándido by the aqueduct is the classic. Also: cathedral, Plaza Mayor, Casa de los Picos.

title: Ávila — Day Trip
summary: Best-preserved medieval walls in Spain. Saint Teresa's birthplace. 1h 10 by train.
content: Train from Chamartín or Príncipe Pío: 1h 10–20. The 11th-century walls (2,500m, 88 towers) encircle the old town — you can walk the parapet. The cathedral is built into the walls. Famous for 'yemas de Santa Teresa' (sweet egg-yolk candies). Quieter than Toledo or Segovia.

title: San Lorenzo de El Escorial — Day Trip
summary: 16th-century monastery-palace, Philip II's massive memorial. 1h by bus/train.
content: Cercanías train from Atocha or Chamartín to El Escorial: ~1h. The monastery is austere, vast, and packed with art (El Greco, Titian, Velázquez, Bosch). Includes the basilica, royal pantheon (Spanish kings since Charles I), library, and the famous 'El Greco rooms'. Combine with a walk in the surrounding mountains.

title: Mercado de San Miguel
summary: Glass-walled market next to Plaza Mayor. Tapas stalls, vermut, oysters, jamón.
content: Open daily 10 AM to midnight (later on weekends). Touristy but genuinely good. Order at any stall, eat standing up at the shared high-tops. The jamón ibérico and the oyster/Champagne stand are standouts. Expect to pay €15–25/person for a full tapas crawl.

title: Chocolatería San Ginés
summary: Madrid's iconic churros-and-chocolate spot, open since 1894. 24/7.
content: Pasadizo de San Ginés, 5 (off Calle Arenal, near Puerta del Sol). Order 'churros con chocolate' — six churros €4.20–4.80, hot chocolate thick enough to stand a churro in. Cash and card both. The marble tables and tuxedoed waiters haven't changed in 30 years. No reservations, queue moves fast.

title: Tapas in La Latina
summary: Madrid's classic tapa crawl. Calle Cava Baja and the surrounding streets.
content: Start at Plaza de la Cebada and walk up Calle Cava Baja. Stops: El Viajero (terrace), Casa Lucas, Taberna Txakolina (Basque), Bodega de la Ardosa (historic, vermouth on tap), Almendro 13 (free tapa with every drink). Sunday El Rastro market spills into the area — arrive before 13:00 to dodge crowds.

title: Vermouth Hour in Madrid
summary: 12:00–13:00, the traditional 'aperitivo' hour. Vermut on tap with a free tapa.
content: A Madrid institution. The classic serve: vermut rojo (red, sweet) on tap, a slice of orange, an olive, a 'puntilla' anchovy, and a small tapa. Best places: Bodega de la Ardosa (Malasaña), Taberna La Concha (Lavapiés), La Venencia (next stop). Order it in Spanish: 'un vermut, por favor.'

title: Flamenco — How to Experience It
summary: Madrid's tablaos are tourist-friendly but serious. Cardamomo, Corral de la Morería, and smaller peñas are the real thing.
content: Tablaos (paid venues): Cardamomo (€35–45 show only, Huertas), Corral de la Morería (€50 show only, La Latina — Michelin-starred kitchen), Torres Bermejas, Las Tablas. Peña flamenca (free or cheap, members + guests): Peña El Flamenco, Peña La Platería, Peña Torres Macarena. Shows run 21:00–23:00. Reserve ahead in summer.

title: Flamenco at Cardamomo
summary: Top tablao in Huertas. Tourist-friendly but the dancers are real.
content: Calle de Echegaray, 15. Shows nightly, dinner + show €60–80, show only €35–45. Two drinks minimum. Reserve ahead in summer. The venue is small and the seating is close to the stage — the experience is intense. Not the most authentic flamenco in town (that's Corral de la Morería or a peña), but reliable and high-quality.

title: Flamenco at Corral de la Morería
summary: Often called the most authentic tablao in Madrid. Michelin-starred paella + flamenco.
content: Calle de la Morería, 17. The only flamenco venue with a Michelin star (for the kitchen). Dinner + show €90–150, show only €50. Respected by dancers as one of the most serious. 10 min from La Latina metro. Open nightly.

title: Teatro Real
summary: Spain's premier opera house, opposite the Royal Palace. Tour €12 or ticket €30–200.
content: Plaza de Isabel II. One of the best acoustics in Europe. Tours in English (45 min) several times daily. Main season Sept–July. Last-minute tickets (€15–30) go on sale 2 hours before each performance — 'Día de la Opera' web page lists them. If opera isn't your thing, do the tour anyway — the building is gorgeous.

title: Café Central — Jazz
summary: Madrid's most famous jazz club. 4 nights a week, two sets each night.
content: Plaza del Ángel, 10. Cover €10–25 depending on the artist. Two sets nightly at 21:00 and 22:30. Order a drink at the bar (minimum) and stay for the show. Bookings recommended in summer. The acoustics are great — focus is on the music, not the food.

title: Malasaña Bars
summary: Indie rock, vinyl bars, dive cocktail joints. Plaza del 2 de Mayo and around.
content: Start at Plaza Dos de Mayo and spiral out. Classics: La Vía Láctea (vinyl, indie), Tupperware (cocktails in takeout containers), El Tigre (giant free tapas with every drink), La Musa (calle de las Madres). Late-night food at Casa María (Casa Maderas) or the kebab places on Fuencarral. Most bars don't get going until 23:00.

title: Chueca Cocktails
summary: Design cocktail bars and LGBT+ venues. Plaza de Chueca and Calle Augusto Figueroa.
content: Top cocktails: Salmon Guru (twice in the World's 50 Best), Bendito, Le Boutique, Viva Madrid (classic Madrileño vermouth joint). LGBT+ circuit: Black & White, Boite, Why Not? Most cocktail bars €12–18 per drink. Salmon Guru has a no-photos policy and a serious cocktail program.

title: La Latina Verbenas
summary: Open-air street parties in August. La Paloma, San Lorenzo, San Cayetano.
content: Three consecutive verbenas in neighbouring barrios: San Cayetano (Rastro, Aug 5–8), San Lorenzo (Lavapiés, Aug 9–12), La Paloma (La Latina, Aug 14–17). Free entry, stages on the plazas, chotis dancing, food stalls. Wear a mantón (shawl) if you want to go native. Plan to arrive after 22:00 for the live music.

title: Real Madrid — Santiago Bernabéu
summary: The Bernabéu stadium tour and match tickets. LaLiga season starts Aug 2026.
content: Stadium tour: €30 (includes museum, dressing room, pitch, trophy room). 1.5 hours. Book online. Match tickets: €50–250 for LaLiga, €100–500+ for Champions League. Buy on realmadrid.com (the only official site). The 2026/27 LaLiga season starts the weekend of 22 Aug 2026. Avoid the stadium area on match day — it gets chaotic.

title: Santiago Bernabéu Stadium
summary: Real Madrid's home since 1947. 78,000+ capacity. Recently renovated with a retractable pitch and 360° video screen.
content: Tour (€30, 1.5 hours): museum, dressing room, presidential box, players' tunnel, pitch-side, panoramic view, trophy room. Match days: arrive 2 hours early, the area around the stadium is hectic. Metro: Santiago Bernabéu (Line 10). The 2026/27 LaLiga opener is the weekend of 22 August 2026.

title: Atlético de Madrid — Cívitas Metropolitano
summary: Atlético's stadium (since 2017), east of the city. 68,000 capacity.
content: Stadium tour: €20. Match tickets €40–200. Metro: Estadio Metropolitano (Line 7). The atmosphere is famously more intense than at the Bernabéu — Atlético fans are known for 'no pasarán' chants. Atlético is more working-class and the stadium experience reflects that. Also hosts concerts in summer.

title: Rayo Vallecano
summary: The people's club of Vallecas. Tiny stadium, huge atmosphere.
content: Estadio de Vallecas, 14,700 capacity. Tickets €15–80. Metro: Portazgo or Buenos Aires. The cheapest authentic football experience in the city. Rayo is known for left-wing fan culture and supporter ownership — banners, flares, working-class pride. Tickets often sold out — buy from the club website on the release date.

title: El Rastro Sunday Market
summary: Europe's biggest flea market. Every Sun + public holidays, 9 AM to 3 PM.
content: Calle de la Ribera de Curtidores and surrounding streets. ~3,500 stalls: vintage clothes, antiques, leather, vinyl, knock-offs, handcrafts. Cash only is safest. Arrive before 11:00 to browse, after 13:00 to haggle. Watch your pockets. Adjacent bars fill up — Bar La Concha is a classic stop.

title: Salamanca District Shopping
summary: Madrid's 'Golden Mile.' Calle Serrano, Goya, Velázquez. Luxury flagships.
content: Hermès, Louis Vuitton, Loewe, Cartier, Chanel — all on Calle Serrano. Quieter and more elegant than Gran Vía. Combine with a coffee at Café Gijón (a Hemingway haunt). Best for window-shopping even if you don't buy.

title: Las Ventas Bullring
summary: Madrid's 23,000-seat bullring. Season runs March to October. Tiles cover the bull names by season.
content: Plaza de Toros de las Ventas, Metro: Ventas. Corridas most Sundays and during the San Isidro Festival (May 15 – June 15). Tickets €20–200, sold at the ring or via entradastorosmadrid.com. The Museo Taurino in the ring is worth a separate visit (€6). Bullfighting is controversial in Spain — the regional ban push is real but Las Ventas is unaffected as of 2026.

title: Spanish Traditions in Madrid
summary: Flamenco, bullfighting, verbenas, chotis, mantones, siesta — a guide to what's still real and what's become a tourist show.
content: Still real: verbenas (street parties, August especially), Sunday family lunch, late dinners (21:00+), tapas culture, religious processions during Semana Santa. Tourist-fied but still worth seeing: bullfighting at Las Ventas (Mar–Oct), flamenco tablaos (the small peñas are more authentic). Mostly myth now: daily siesta (urban Madrileños don't do this; small shops in some barrios might close 14:00–17:00).

title: Veranos de la Villa
summary: Madrid's biggest summer festival. 2026 edition: 7 July – 30 August, 14 districts, 72 events.
content: Concerts, theatre, dance, zarzuela, circus, family programming. Most events €5–25, many free. Spread across the city: Conde Duque, Matadero, Fernán Gómez, Teatro Real (open-air), Plaza de España. Check veranosdelavilla.com for the full programme. Reservations open 2 weeks before each event.

title: August Verbenas (Street Parties)
summary: Three back-to-back street festivals: San Cayetano, San Lorenzo, La Paloma.
content: 2026 dates: San Cayetano 5–8 Aug (Rastro/Embajadores), San Lorenzo 9–12 Aug (Lavapiés), La Paloma 14–17 Aug (La Latina). All free, with stages on the main plazas, food stalls, and traditional chotis dancing. The biggest is La Paloma. Wear comfortable shoes — the streets are packed from 21:00 onwards.

title: Madrid Pride (MADO)
summary: Europe's largest Pride. Late June to early July. The parade on Saturday is the main event.
content: MADO (Madrid Orgullo) runs 10 days. Plaza de Chueca and surroundings are the centre. Saturday parade floats through Gran Vía to Plaza de Colón. Free to watch, paid tickets for concerts and the closing party. Dates: 28 Jun – 5 Jul 2026 (TBC). The party goes until 6 AM during the week of Pride — book accommodation early.

title: Live Madrid Time
summary: Europe/Madrid (CEST UTC+2 summer, CET UTC+1 winter). Same time as Paris and Berlin.
content: Spain uses Central European Time. No daylight saving confusion in 2026 — the EU voted to scrap the time change but implementation is delayed. Lunch is typically 14:00–16:00, dinner starts 21:00. Most shops don't open before 10:00. Siesta is largely a myth for urban Madrileños, but many smaller shops close 14:00–17:00.

title: Madrid Weather
summary: Hot dry summers (30–38°C Jul–Aug), cold winters (0–10°C Dec–Feb). Spring and autumn are mild.
content: Summer: 14h daylight, intense midday sun, cool evenings. Bring sunscreen, water, light layers. Winter: short days (sunset ~17:30), occasional snow but rare. Best months: April, May, September, October. Madrid is dry year-round — humidity rarely above 60%. AEMET (the national met service) has the most accurate forecast.

title: Madrid Air Quality
summary: Generally good. Ozone spikes in summer afternoons. Winter NO2 from traffic and heating.
content: Real-time AQI: consultaq.opendata.es. Stations: Plaza de España, Cuatro Caminos, Retiro. Madrid has a 'Madrid 360' plan to ban polluting cars from the city centre by 2025 — check the 'Madrid Central' / 'Madrid 360' rules if you're driving. The Manzanares river corridor is the cleanest area; the M-30 ring road the worst.

title: Metro Closures
summary: Summer 2026: planned closures on Lines 6, 7, 9B, 10. Free shuttle buses cover affected sections.
content: The Comunidad de Madrid does major track and station work in August when ridership drops. Summer 2026 affected: Line 9B (TFM) Arganda-Rivas closed 25 Jul – 13 Aug. Line 6 Argüelles-Ciudad Universitaria closed 1 Aug – 2 Sep (Moncloa station work). Line 7 Gregorio Marañón-Pitis closed 2–28 Aug. Line 10 Plaza Castilla-Nuevos Ministerios extended to 21 Aug (Bernabéu renovation). All affected sections have free EMT shuttle buses.

title: Prado — Current Exhibitions
summary: Rotating exhibitions in the Jerónimos building. Check the official site for what's showing now.
content: The Prado's temporary exhibitions are world-class and usually have an English-language audio guide. Timed entry required. Many sell out 2–3 weeks ahead. Combine with the permanent collection for a full day. EU students under 25 free with ID.

title: Reina Sofía — Current Exhibitions
summary: Modern and contemporary art. Recent retrospectives have included Dalí, Picasso, and feminist art movements.
content: Two main temporary slots per year. Sabatini Building is the main venue. Combine a visit with the current 'Colección' (which rotates selections from the permanent collection) to see less-crowded Picasso, Miró, and Dalí works.

title: Thyssen — Current Exhibitions
summary: Temporary shows often focus on a single artist or movement, in the ground-floor Villahermosa wing.
content: The Thyssen's temporary programming is more focused than the Prado's. Recent examples: Edward Hopper, Van Gogh, Bauhaus, Russian avant-garde. Audio guide in English. Plan 2–3 hours including the permanent collection.

title: El País (English edition)
summary: Spain's largest newspaper, daily English edition. The best source for current Madrid news.
content: The English edition is selected translations of the Spanish front pages. Free tier covers most news. Has a dedicated 'Madrid' section. Useful for finding out about protests, strikes, and city updates that affect your plans.

title: Madrid Daily in English
summary: English-language expat news, events, and classifieds. Founded 2002.
content: MadridDaily.com (independent) and The Local Spain (thelocal.es) are both long-running. Expat-oriented content, jobs, rentals, events. Less authoritative than El País but better for day-to-day practical questions.

title: Housing in Madrid
summary: Apartments, neighborhoods, contracts, and deposits. Everything you need to find and keep a flat in Madrid.
content: Search portals: Idealista and Fotocasa are the two main property sites. Pick a barrio first — Malasaña (indie, lively), Chamberí (traditional, families), Salamanca (upscale, business), Retiro (parks, family), Chueca (design, LGBT+), Lavapiés (multicultural, edgy, cheaper), La Latina (old-town tapas), Arganzuela (up-and-coming, near Madrid Río). Deposits: 1 month standard ('fianza'), plus often a 1-month 'aval' bank guarantee. Contracts are usually 11 months. Get a written 'inventario' (inventory) at move-in. Avoid paying anything before viewing in person — scams are common. For short stays: look at Spotahome, Badi, or local Facebook groups.

title: Healthcare in Madrid
summary: Public (Seguridad Social), private (Sanitas/Adeslas), and English-speaking options. Plus pharmacies.
content: Three systems: (1) Public — Seguridad Social, free or low-cost, requires being on the padron and either working or paying the 'convenio especial' (~€60/month). Get a 'tarjeta sanitaria' at your local Centro de Salud. (2) Private — Sanitas, Adeslas, DKV, IMQ. €50–200/month. Faster, English-speaking, more choice. (3) Tourist — pay upfront or use travel insurance. For emergencies, any public hospital ER treats you regardless. Pharmacies (green cross) are everywhere; every district has a 'farmacia de guardia' open 24h. English-speaking clinics: Madrid Healthcare, Hospital HM Sanchinarro, British-American Clinic.

title: Banking in Madrid
summary: Spanish bank accounts, transfers, cards, ATMs, and the NIE-paperwork connection.
content: Big 5 banks: CaixaBank, BBVA, Santander, Bankinter, Sabadell. To open a Spanish account you need: NIE, empadronamiento certificate, employment contract or self-employed status, sometimes a minimum deposit. Online-friendly options: Wise, N26, Revolut — work for non-residents and accepted most places. ATM tip: avoid Euronet machines in tourist areas; use bank-affiliated ATMs. Card acceptance: Visa/Mastercard everywhere, Amex only in hotels and big chains. For non-EU residents, opening a Spanish account is essential for paying rent, utilities, and receiving salary. Cash still common in small bars, taxis, and market stalls.

title: Ride-Sharing (Uber & Cabify)
summary: Uber and Cabify both operate in Madrid. Cabify (Spanish company) is often cheaper and more popular with locals.
content: Both Uber and Cabify operate legally in Madrid. Cabify is a Spanish company and tends to be the preferred choice for locals — often slightly cheaper than Uber and with better Spanish-language support. Both apps work in English. Pick-up points at the airport are signed (follow 'VCN' or 'Ride App' signs). Surge pricing applies during peak hours and rainy days. Payment is cashless via the app. Licensed Madrid taxis can also be booked via the PideTaxi or FreeNow apps.

items.title: Cabify
items.body: Spanish company, popular with locals. Often cheaper. English app.

items.title: Uber
items.body: Available but slightly more expensive. English app.

items.title: FreeNow
items.body: Books licensed Madrid taxis (white with red diagonal stripe). Useful when surge pricing hits Uber/Cabify.

sections.heading: Airport pick-up
sections.body: At Barajas, follow signs to 'Ride App' or 'VCN' (VTC) pick-up zones. Each terminal has a designated pick-up point — check your app for the exact zone letter. Taxis have a fixed €30 fare to central Madrid (inside the M-30), which is often cheaper than ride-sharing for airport runs.

title: BiciMAD — Electric Bike Sharing
summary: Madrid's electric bike-share system. 6,000+ e-bikes across 250+ stations. Great for short trips in the flat center.
content: BiciMAD is Madrid's municipal electric bike-sharing system, operated by EMT (the same company that runs the buses). There are over 6,000 electric bikes at 250+ stations across the city. The center of Madrid is mostly flat and very bikeable, though some outer areas are hilly. The bikes have pedal-assist so hills are manageable. You need the BiciMAD app (English available) to unlock bikes — no magnetic card needed anymore. Annual membership is €25/year for residents, or pay-per-use at €0.10/min. Helmets are not required by law in Spanish cities but recommended.

items.title: Download the app
items.body: Get the BiciMAD app (English available). Create an account with your phone number.

items.title: Add credit or a pass
items.body: Pay-per-use needs a credit card on file. Annual pass is €25 for residents, €50 for non-residents.

items.title: Find a bike
items.body: Use the app map to find a station with available bikes. Tap 'Unlock' in the app and scan the QR code on the bike.

items.title: Return the bike
items.body: Dock at any BiciMAD station. The app confirms return and charges your account.

sections.heading: Best for short trips
sections.body: BiciMAD is ideal for trips under 3 km in the center — Retiro Park, Madrid Río, the historic core, and between museums. Avoid the Gran Vía during rush hour (heavy traffic and tram tracks). The Madrid Río park has dedicated bike lanes and is a lovely ride.

title: Electric Scooters (Lime, Dott, Tier)
summary: Shared e-scooters from Lime, Dott, and Tier operate in central Madrid. €1 to unlock + €0.15/min.
content: Three e-scooter operators are licensed in the Madrid city center: Lime, Dott, and Tier. They're convenient for short trips but the city has strict rules: no riding on sidewalks (€200 fine), no riding in Retiro Park or historic pedestrian zones, and you must park in designated areas shown in the app. Helmets are recommended but not required. The scooters are geofenced to disable outside the service area. Best for the 1-3 km trips that are too long to walk but too short to bother with the metro.

title: Walking in Madrid
summary: Madrid's compact center is one of Europe's most walkable cities. Most sights within 20-30 min walk of Puerta del Sol.
content: Madrid's historic center is extremely walkable — most major sights are within a 20-30 minute walk of Puerta del Sol (the city's kilometer zero). The center is mostly flat, with wide pedestrianized streets (Calles Mayor, Arenal, Preciados) and numerous parks. Walking is the best way to discover the city's hidden plazas, tapas bars, and street art. Avoid walking alone in poorly-lit areas late at night (Lavapiés and Cuatro Caminos after midnight). The official Madrid tourism site has 7 self-guided walking routes (1-3 hours each) covering the Habsburg city, the Bourbons, literary Madrid, and more.

title: Airport Express Bus (Line 203)
summary: 24/7 bus between Barajas Airport and Atocha/Cibeles. €5, 30-40 min. Best for late-night arrivals when Metro is closed.
content: EMT line 203 (the Airport Express) runs 24/7 between Barajas Airport and central Madrid, terminating at Atocha station (with stops at Cibeles and O'Donnell along the way). It's the only public transport option between 1:30 AM (when Metro closes) and 6 AM. Buses run every 15-20 minutes during the day, every 35 minutes at night. The bus stops at all four airport terminals (T1, T2, T3, T4) — check the sign for your terminal. Luggage goes under the bus (free). Pay the driver with cash (exact change appreciated) or use a Metrobús card. The bus is wheelchair accessible.

title: Private Airport Transfer Services
summary: Pre-booked private transfers from Barajas to your hotel. €35-60. Best for groups, families, or arrivals with lots of luggage.
content: If you don't want to deal with public transport after a long flight, several companies offer pre-booked private transfers from Barajas Airport to your Madrid accommodation. The driver meets you at arrivals with a name sign. Prices are flat-rate (€35-60 for a sedan, €70-100 for a van) and include all taxes, tolls, and tips. Booking in advance is required — they don't cruise the airport like taxis. Trip time is 20-30 minutes to the center (traffic dependent). Popular companies include Madrid Airport Transfer, Suntransfers, and Welcome Pickups. For comparison, a licensed Madrid taxi costs a flat €30 to central Madrid (inside the M-30), and Uber/Cabify costs €25-35.

title: Airlines Serving Madrid-Barajas
summary: Iberia (hub), Air Europa, Vueling, Ryanair. Direct flights to 80+ international destinations.
content: Madrid-Barajas is a major European hub with direct flights to over 80 international destinations. Iberia is the flag carrier and uses Barajas as its hub (Terminals 4 and 4S). Air Europa is the second-largest Spanish carrier. Low-cost carriers Vueling, Ryanair, and EasyJet serve European routes from Terminals 1 and 2. Long-haul flights to the Americas are concentrated at T4S (satellite terminal — take the underground train from T4). For the best fares, book 6-8 weeks ahead for European flights, 2-3 months for long-haul. Iberia and Air Europa are part of the Oneworld and SkyTeam alliances respectively, so you can earn miles on partner airlines.

title: AVE High-Speed Trains
summary: Europe's largest high-speed rail network. Madrid to Barcelona 2h30, Seville 2h30, Valencia 1h40. From €20 one-way.
content: Spain has the largest high-speed rail network in Europe and the second-largest in the world (after China). The AVE (Alta Velocidad Española) connects Madrid to major Spanish cities at up to 310 km/h. Trains depart from Atocha station (Puerta de Atocha) for most destinations, and from Chamartín for Galicia and Asturias. Fares start at €20 one-way if booked in advance (up to 60 days ahead) but rise to €150+ for last-minute bookings. The cheapest fares are non-refundable 'Promo' tickets. Renfe's competing low-cost brand 'Ouigo' (French operator) and 'Iryo' (private Spanish operator) offer even cheaper fares on the Madrid-Barcelona and Madrid-Valencia routes. Trains are comfortable, with power outlets, WiFi (spotty), and a café car.

sections.heading: Cheaper alternatives
sections.body: Ouigo (ouigo.com) and Iryo (iryo.eu) compete with Renfe on the Madrid-Barcelona and Madrid-Valencia routes. Fares start at €9 one-way. Slightly less legroom and luggage allowance than AVE, but the same travel time.

title: Intercity Buses (Madrid to other cities)
summary: Cheaper than trains for intercity travel. Madrid to Granada 5h (€25), Lisbon 8h (€45), Bilbao 4h30 (€22).
content: Intercity buses are the budget alternative to trains for travel within Spain and to neighboring countries. They're slower but often half the price of the AVE. The main operator is ALSA (owned by National Express), with routes to every major Spanish city and international destinations including Portugal, France, and Morocco (via ferry). Other operators include Avanza (Castilla y León, Extremadura routes) and Daibus (Andalusia). Buses depart from Estación Sur (Méndez Álvaro) for most ALSA routes, and from Moncloa for Avanza routes. Book at alsa.es for the best fares — English site available. Buses are modern, air-conditioned, with WiFi (when it works) and onboard toilets.

title: Bus Stations in Madrid
summary: Estación Sur (Méndez Álvaro) for ALSA intercity. Moncloa for Castilla y León. Plaza Elíptica for south routes.
content: Madrid has several bus stations serving different regions. Estación Sur (also called Méndez Álvaro) is the main intercity station — ALSA routes to Barcelona, Valencia, Andalusia, Portugal, and France all depart from here. Moncloa station serves Castilla y León (Segovia, Ávila, Salamanca) and Galicia routes. Plaza Elíptica handles some southern routes (Toledo, Cuenca). Each station is on a Metro line: Estación Sur on Line 6 (Méndez Álvaro), Moncloa on Lines 3 and 6, Plaza Elíptica on Lines 6 and 11. Arrive 20-30 minutes before departure to find your platform — large stations like Estación Sur have 50+ platforms. Buy tickets in advance online or at station ticket windows.

title: Eurail / Interrail Passes
summary: Multi-country train passes for non-European (Eurail) and European (Interrail) residents. Spain is one pass country.
content: Eurail (for non-European residents) and Interrail (for European residents) passes offer flexible train travel across multiple European countries. A 'Global Pass' covers 33 countries including Spain. A 'One Country Pass' for Spain is also available. Passes give you a set number of travel days within a period (e.g., 3 days within 1 month, 7 days within 1 month, or continuous travel for up to 3 months). High-speed AVE trains in Spain require a seat reservation (€10-20 additional per trip) even with a pass — book these in advance at renfe.com or at Atocha station. The pass is best for travelers visiting 3+ countries or 4+ Spanish cities. For just Madrid + 1-2 other cities, point-to-point tickets booked in advance are usually cheaper.

title: Driving & Car Rental in Madrid
summary: Don't drive in central Madrid (LEZ restrictions, expensive parking). Rent a car only for day trips outside the city.
content: Driving in central Madrid is not recommended for visitors. The city has a Low Emission Zone (ZBE — Zona de Bajas Emisiones) that restricts older vehicles, and parking is expensive (€2-4/hour on-street, €25-40/day in lots) with limited spaces. The historic center has narrow one-way streets and pedestrian zones. Traffic is heavy and Spanish drivers are aggressive. That said, renting a car for day trips OUTSIDE Madrid is worthwhile — destinations like Toledo, Segovia, El Escorial, and the Castilian countryside are easy drives (1 hour each way) and not all are reachable by train. Major rental companies (Avis, Hertz, Europcar, Enterprise) have offices at Barajas Airport and Atocha station. An International Driving Permit is recommended for non-EU licenses. Drive on the right. Speed limits: 50 km/h city, 90 km/h highway, 120 km/h motorway.

title: Parking in Madrid
summary: On-street parking is regulated (SER zones) and expensive. Public lots cost €25-40/day. Most central hotels don't include parking.
content: Parking in central Madrid is challenging and expensive. On-street parking in the SER (Servicio de Estacionamiento Regulado) zones costs €1.05-3.00/hour depending on the zone (blue zones are cheaper, green zones are resident-only). Maximum stay is usually 2 hours. Pay at the meter with a credit card or via the ElParking app. Most central hotels do NOT include parking — expect to pay €25-40/day extra. The cheapest public lots are around Gran Vía (Parking Gran Vía, €28/day) and Plaza Mayor (Parking Plaza Mayor, €30/day). Free parking is virtually nonexistent in the center. If you're staying outside the center, look for hotels near Metro stations with free parking, then take the Metro in. The M-30 ring road has park-and-ride lots at some Metro stations (€3-5/day including Metro tickets for all passengers).

title: SIM Cards & Mobile Plans
summary: Orange, Vodafone, Movistar, and Digi are the main carriers. Prepaid SIMs from €10, monthly plans from €20.
content: Spain has four major mobile carriers: Movistar (largest coverage), Vodafone, Orange, and Digi (budget). All sell prepaid SIM cards (€10-20 with credit) and monthly plans (€20-35 for unlimited data + calls). SIM cards are available at carrier stores (found in every shopping center), phone shops (Phone House), and tobacco shops (estancos). You'll need your passport to buy a SIM. 5G is widely available in Madrid. For short visits, consider an eSIM (Airalo, Holafly) — no need to find a store, download and activate before you land. EU roaming: if you have an EU SIM, you can use it in Spain at no extra charge (fair use limits apply).

title: eSIMs for Spain Travel
summary: Airalo, Holafly, and Maya Mobile offer Spain eSIMs from $5. No physical SIM needed — activate before you land.
content: eSIMs are the easiest way to get mobile data in Spain without finding a store. Buy online, scan a QR code, and you're connected before your plane lands. Three popular providers: Airalo (Spain plan from $5 for 1GB / 7 days, $15 for 5GB / 30 days), Holafly (unlimited data €19 for 5 days, €47 for 15 days), and Maya Mobile (€5 for 1GB / 7 days). All three work on the Movistar and Orange networks. eSIMs are DATA ONLY — no Spanish phone number, no SMS. If you need to receive SMS (for banking 2FA etc.), keep your home SIM active. eSIMs work on iPhone XS and later, and most recent Android phones. Check compatibility before buying.

title: Public Wi-Fi in Madrid
summary: Madrid Free WiFi network covers the center (600+ hotspots). Cafés, libraries, and Metro stations also have free Wi-Fi.
content: Madrid has a municipal free Wi-Fi network ('Madrid WiFi') with 600+ hotspots across the city center, in parks, libraries, sports centers, and public buildings. Connect to the 'Madrid_Free_WiFi' network and accept the terms in your browser. Speed is decent (10-20 Mbps) and there's a 2-hour session limit (reconnect after). Most cafés (Starbucks, McDonald's, Costa) have free Wi-Fi with purchase. The Madrid Metro offers free 'WiFiMetromadrid' at major stations (Sol, Atocha, Chamartín, Gran Vía, etc.). Public libraries (Bibliotecas Públicas Municipales) have free Wi-Fi and free public computers (1-hour sessions with a library card or passport). For remote work, coworking spaces offer the most reliable connections.

title: Coworking Spaces in Madrid
summary: Impact Hub, WeWork, Toni Mora, and others across Madrid. Day passes €20-30, monthly from €120.
content: Madrid has a thriving coworking scene, popular with digital nomads and remote workers. Major chains: WeWork (3 locations: Velázquez, Caleido, Castellana), Impact Hub Madrid (best for social entrepreneurs), and Atenasia. Local favorites: TNT Coworking (Malasaña), Utopic_US (Lavapiés), and Betahaus (Cuatro Caminos). Day passes cost €20-30 (free coffee, fast WiFi, printer access). Monthly dedicated desks from €120-250, private offices from €400. Most spaces offer a free trial day — book ahead. The Malasaña and Salesas neighborhoods have the highest concentration of coworking spaces. For a quieter alternative, the municipal libraries (Biblioteca Pública) offer free Wi-Fi and desks, but no coffee or talking.

title: Internet Cafés in Madrid
summary: Becoming rare due to smartphones and free Wi-Fi. Best bet: Locutorios (call shops) in Lavapiés and Cuatro Caminos.
content: Internet cafés (cibercafés) are increasingly rare in Madrid since most people have smartphones and free public Wi-Fi is widespread. The few that remain are usually combined with locutorios (call shops) in immigrant neighborhoods like Lavapiés, Cuatro Caminos, and Tetuán. They offer computer rental (€1-2/hour), international calling, and printing/scanning services. For printing documents, the chain Point 7 (multiple locations) is reliable. For longer computer sessions, public libraries offer free computer use (1-hour sessions). If you just need Wi-Fi, any café or McDonald's will do. If you need a quiet space to work, see the Coworking Spaces record.

title: International Calling Cards
summary: Largely obsolete due to WhatsApp and VoIP. Locutorios in Lavapiés still sell them for cheap international calls.
content: International calling cards (tarjetas telefónicas) are mostly obsolete now thanks to WhatsApp, Skype, and affordable mobile data. They're still sold in locutorios (call shops) in immigrant neighborhoods like Lavapiés, Cueto Caminos, and Tetuán — mostly for older residents calling family in Latin America, Africa, and Eastern Europe. Cards cost €5-10 and offer 100-300 minutes of international calling. For visitors, the better option is to use WhatsApp (free with any data plan), Skype (€0.02/min to most countries), or your home country's VoIP service. If you must call a Spanish landline from abroad, the country code is +34. Spanish mobile numbers start with 6 or 7 (9 digits), landlines start with 8 or 9 (9 digits).

title: Post Office (Correos)
summary: Correos is Spain's national postal service. Yellow post boxes everywhere, main offices open Mon-Fri 8:30-20:30.
content: Correos is Spain's national postal service, identifiable by its yellow color and blue crown logo. Yellow post boxes (buzones) for mail are on most streets. Main post offices (oficinas principales) are open Mon-Fri 8:30-20:30 and Sat 9:30-14:00. Smaller offices close for lunch (14:00-16:00). The historic main post office is the Palacio de Comunicaciones at Plaza de Cibeles — now the City Hall, but there's still a Correos counter inside. Domestic mail: standard letter €0.55, takes 1-3 days. International letter: €1.35 to Europe, €1.80 elsewhere, takes 5-10 days. For packages, use Correos or private couriers (DHL, UPS, FedEx) — Correos is cheaper but slower. Poste restante (lista de correos) is available at main offices if you don't have a fixed address.

title: Launderettes & Dry Cleaners
summary: Self-service launderettes (lavanderías) €5-8 per load. Dry cleaners (tintorerías) on every corner.
content: Self-service launderettes (lavanderías autoservicio) are common in central Madrid, especially in tourist and student areas (Malasaña, Lavapiés, Chamberí). Expect €5-8 for a wash (30-40 min cycle), €3-5 extra for dryer (15-20 min). Most take coins; some have an app. Wash & fold service (lavandería con servicio) costs €10-15 per load — drop off in the morning, pick up in the evening. Dry cleaners (tintorerías) are everywhere — Speedy and Tinto are common chains. Dry cleaning a shirt costs €4-6, a suit €12-18. Most hotels offer laundry service but it's expensive (€5-10 per item). For longer stays, consider using a wash & fold service near your accommodation.

title: Locksmiths (Cerrajeros)
summary: 24/7 emergency locksmiths (cerrajeros 24 horas). Be careful — some quote low then charge €200+ for 'extra work'.
content: Locksmiths (cerrajeros) are easy to find in Madrid — Google 'cerrajero 24 horas [your neighborhood]' for an emergency callout. However, this is one of Madrid's most notorious scam industries. Common tactic: quote €50-70 on the phone, then charge €200-400 'because the lock was harder than expected' or 'we had to drill it'. To avoid this: (1) get a firm quote in writing before they start, (2) ask if the quote includes all labor and parts, (3) refuse to pay more than the quoted price, (4) if they refuse to leave without more money, call the police (091). For non-emergencies, ask your building's portero (doorman) or a local hardware store (ferretería) for a recommendation — they usually know reliable cerrajeros. Lockouts during business hours: try your building's portero first, who often has a master key.

title: Moving & Shipping Services
summary: Local movers €300-600 for a 1-bedroom apartment. International shipping: SeptembertoMove, Glovo Express for boxes.
content: For local moves within Madrid, expect €300-600 for a 1-bedroom apartment (3-4 hours, 2 movers, van). Reliable companies include Mudanzas Madrid, Movingbox, and Fuenmov. Book 2-3 weeks ahead, especially at end-of-month (most leases end on the 30th). Most buildings require you to reserve the service elevator (montacargas) and book a time slot — your building's portero arranges this. For international shipping, SeptembertoMove and Crown Relocations specialize in expat moves. For sending boxes (books, clothes, etc.) within Spain or internationally, the cheapest options are Correos Paq Standard (€15-30 for 5kg within Spain) or Seur (€25-40). For sending furniture, use Tictactrip or Glovo Express. Self-storage (trasteros) costs €50-150/month depending on size — Accessa and City Trastero are common chains.

title: Translating & Interpreting Services
summary: Sworn translators (traductores jurados) for official documents €30-60/page. Interpreters €30-50/hour.
content: For official documents (birth certificates, marriage certificates, contracts), you need a 'traductor jurado' (sworn translator) certified by the Spanish Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Their translations are legally valid. Rates: €30-60 per page for English-Spanish, more for less common languages. Find one at exteriores.gob.es (the official list of sworn translators). For non-official translation (websites, emails, casual documents), standard translators charge €0.08-0.12 per word. For in-person interpreting (doctors appointments, legal meetings, real estate viewings), expect €30-50/hour with a 2-hour minimum. For informal help (visas, paperwork), intercambios (language exchanges) are a good place to find bilingual Spanish/English speakers who can help informally. The Madrid American Women's Club and other expat groups often have translation recommendations.

title: Barbers & Hairdressers
summary: Men's haircut €15-25, women's €30-60. Look for 'Peluquería' (unisex/women) or 'Barbería' (men).
content: Madrid has thousands of hair salons (peluquerías) and barbers (barberías). For men, traditional barbers (barbería) charge €15-25 for a haircut and shave, often with a beer included. Good chains: Barbería Madrid, The Old Barber, and L3B. For women, expect €30-60 at a mid-range peluquería and €80-150 at a high-end one. Common mid-range chains: Peluquería Kameleon, Marco Aldany. For budget options, the 'Low Cost' chain charges €12-15 for women's cuts. Most salons take walk-ins but booking ahead is recommended on weekends. Tipping is not expected (but appreciated — round up to the nearest €5). For Black hair care, Madrid has a small but growing number of specialist salons in the Cuatro Caminos and Tetuán neighborhoods. For English-speaking stylists, ask in expat Facebook groups — there are several British and American stylists working in Salamanca and Chamberí.

title: Sol & Gran Vía (City Center)
summary: Madrid's tourist heart. Puerta del Sol, Plaza Mayor, Gran Vía shopping street, Royal Palace. Busy day and night.
content: Sol-Gran Vía is Madrid's tourist and commercial center. Puerta del Sol is the city's kilometer zero and the busiest square in Spain. From here, walk 5 minutes west to Plaza Mayor (17th-century arcaded square), 10 minutes to the Royal Palace, or north up Gran Vía (Madrid's Broadway — theaters, cinemas, shopping, and the famous Telefonica building). The neighborhood is packed with tourists by day and clubbers by night. It's well-connected: Sol Metro (Lines 1, 2, 3 + Renfe Cercanías), Gran Vía Metro (Lines 1, 5), and Ópera Metro (Lines 2, 5 + Ramal). Best for: first-time visitors, shopping, theater, sightseeing. Avoid: budget accommodation (overpriced and noisy), tapas chains (overpriced and bad). Eat at Calle de la Cruz or Calle de las Infantas instead.

title: Malasaña (Alternative & Hipster)
summary: Madrid's hipster heart. Vintage shops, craft beer, indie cafés, alternative nightlife. Birthplace of La Movida.
content: Malasaña is Madrid's alternative and hipster neighborhood, located just north of Gran Vía. It was the epicenter of La Movida Madrileña (the cultural explosion after Franco's death in the 1980s) and still has a rebellious, creative spirit. Calle Fuencarral is the main shopping street (pedestrianized, mostly chain stores). For independent shops, explore Calle del Pez, Calle del Espíritu Santo, and Plaza del Dos de Mayo (the neighborhood's main square, with a statue of local hero Daoíz). Best for: vintage shopping, craft beer, specialty coffee, vegetarian food, indie bookstores, alternative nightlife (rock, indie, electro). Avoid: weekends after midnight — gets very crowded and noisy. Metro: Bilbao (L1, L4), San Bernardo (L2, L4), Noviciado (L2).

title: Chueca (LGBTQ+ & Chic)
summary: Madrid's LGBTQ+ neighborhood. Chic boutiques, cocktail bars, San Antón market. Pride (MADO) is the big annual event.
content: Chueca is Madrid's LGBTQ+ neighborhood, located between Gran Vía and Malasaña. Named after Federico Chueca (the 19th-century composer), it became the center of gay life in the 1980s and is now one of the most open and welcoming neighborhoods in Europe. The main square is Plaza de Chueca, surrounded by café terraces. Calle Hortaleza and Calle Fuencarral are the main shopping streets (boutiques, design stores, the Mercado de San Antón food market). Best for: chic shopping, cocktail bars, brunch, drag shows, LGBTQ+ nightlife (especially Calle Pelayo and Calle Libertad). Madrid Pride (MADO) at the end of June / early July is one of the largest Pride celebrations in Europe — the neighborhood is the epicenter. Metro: Chueca (L5), Gran Vía (L1, L5).

title: Salamanca (Upscale & Luxury)
summary: Madrid's wealthiest neighborhood. Golden Mile luxury shopping (Calle Serrano), embassies, fine dining, calm residential streets.
content: Salamanca is Madrid's most exclusive neighborhood, developed in the 19th century by the Marquis of Salamanca. Wide tree-lined streets, modernist buildings, and the city's most expensive real estate. Calle Serrano (the 'Golden Mile') has luxury flagships: Loewe, Hermès, Prada, Gucci, Louis Vuitton. Calle Jorge Juan and Calle Ayala have independent boutiques and gourmet food shops. The neighborhood is home to many embassies (US, UK, Germany, France), the National Archaeological Museum, and the Lázaro Galdiano Museum. Best for: luxury shopping, fine dining, calm walks, business travelers (many hotels). Avoid: if you're on a budget — this is the most expensive part of Madrid. Metro: Serrano (L4), Velázquez (L4), República Argentina (L6).

title: La Latina (Tapas & Tradition)
summary: Madrid's oldest neighborhood. Tapas bars on Cava Baja, El Rastro flea market on Sundays, traditional vermouth culture.
content: La Latina is one of Madrid's oldest neighborhoods, founded in the medieval period. It's the spiritual home of Madrid tapas culture. Calle Cava Baja and Calle Cava Alta are lined with tapas bars — go on a Saturday afternoon and bar-hop your way through. The Sunday flea market El Rastro (9am-3pm) spreads through the streets around Plaza de Cascorro — Madrid's largest open-air market. La Latina is also where you'll find the city's best traditional vermouth (vermut de grifo) at places like Casa La Latina and La Concha. Best for: tapas, Sunday Rastro, traditional Madrid atmosphere, nightlife that starts at lunch. Avoid: Sunday afternoon if you hate crowds. Metro: La Latina (L5), Tirso de Molina (L1).

title: Lavapiés (Multicultural & Bohemian)
summary: Madrid's most diverse neighborhood. Indian curry, Senegalese music, flamenco caves, alternative theaters, street art.
content: Lavapiés is Madrid's most multicultural neighborhood, located south of the center. It has the highest concentration of foreign-born residents in Madrid — Indian, Pakistani, Senegalese, Moroccan, Chinese, and Filipino communities all coexist here. The result is one of Europe's most exciting food scenes: Calle Lavapiés has Indian curry houses, the south side has Senegalese and West African restaurants, and there are excellent Middle Eastern and Asian options. It's also a bohemian neighborhood with alternative theaters (Teatro del Barrio, Sala Cuarta Pared), street art, and flamenco venues (Casa Patas, Cardamomo). Best for: international food, alternative culture, affordable accommodation, photography. Avoid: late at night alone — the neighborhood has a reputation (often overstated) for petty crime. Metro: Lavapiés (L3), Tirso de Molina (L1).

title: Chamberí (Authentic Local)
summary: Authentic residential neighborhood. Local tapas, the Andén 0 Metro museum, calm tree-lined streets. Less touristy than center.
content: Chamberí is an authentic residential neighborhood just north of Malasaña, popular with middle-class madrileños. It's a great base for visitors who want to experience 'real Madrid' without giving up central convenience. The main drag is Calle Fuencarral (which runs from Gran Vía all the way up to Chamberí). The neighborhood has excellent tapas around Plaza de Olavide and Calle Cardenal Cisneros. The Andén 0 museum (free) is a preserved 1919 Metro station — you can see the old tilework and a train. Other attractions: the Sorolla Museum (the painter's house and garden), the Santa Rita church, and the Mercado de Chamberí (covered market). Best for: a quieter base, local tapas, family-friendly accommodation. Metro: Bilbao (L1, L4), Alonso Martínez (L1, L4, L5, L10), Chamberí (L1, L2).

title: Retiro (Parks & Museums)
summary: Upscale residential around Retiro Park. Prado, Reina Sofía, Thyssen museums. Calm, family-friendly, near the center.
content: The Retiro neighborhood (Jerónimos) is the upscale residential area around Retiro Park, east of Paseo del Prado. It's home to the 'Golden Triangle of Art' — the Prado, Reina Sofía, and Thyssen-Bornemisza museums. The 118-hectare Retiro Park itself is the neighborhood's heart: rowing lake, Crystal Palace, Rose Garden, thousands of trees. The neighborhood has elegant 19th-century architecture, the Puerta de Alcalá, the National Library, and the Madrid Stock Exchange. Best for: museum visits, park days, family accommodation, jogging, quiet sophistication. Avoid: budget travelers — accommodation and restaurants are expensive here. Calle Moratín has the best tapas in the area. Metro: Retiro (L2), Banco de España (L2), Atocha (L1, R + Renfe).

title: Argüelles & Moncloa (Students & Parks)
summary: Student-heavy neighborhood near Parque del Oeste and Temple of Debod. Best sunset views in Madrid.
content: Argüelles and Moncloa are adjacent neighborhoods in western central Madrid, popular with students (the Complutense University is nearby) and known for parks. Parque del Oeste has rose gardens, an old cable car (Teleférico) to Casa de Campo, and the Temple of Debod — an ancient Egyptian temple gifted to Spain, with the best sunset views in Madrid. The Teleférico cable car runs from here to Casa de Campo (€6 one-way, €11 round trip). Moncloa is also the main transport hub for buses to El Escorial, Ávila, and Segovia (the Moncloa bus station is here). Best for: sunset at Temple of Debod, students, budget accommodation, day trips. Metro: Argüelles (L3, L4, L6), Moncloa (L3, L6), Ventura Rodríguez (L3).

title: Tetuán & Cuatro Caminos (Diverse & Local)
summary: Diverse working-class neighborhood north of center. African markets, authentic tapas, cheaper rent.
content: Tetuán and Cuatro Caminos are diverse working-class neighborhoods north of Chamberí. They have a large immigrant population (especially West African, Latin American, and Filipino) and a strong local character. The Mercado de Maravillas on Calle Bravo Murillo is one of Madrid's largest covered markets — three floors of fresh produce, fish, meat, and tapas bars. Calle Orense (just west of the Paseo de la Castellana) is one of Madrid's main shopping streets, less touristy than Gran Vía. The neighborhoods are more affordable than central areas — accommodation and restaurants are 20-30% cheaper. Best for: budget travelers, authentic local life, food markets, African hair salons and shops. Avoid: alone at night in poorly-lit side streets. Metro: Cuatro Caminos (L1, L2, L6), Tetuán (L1), Estrecho (L1).

title: Hop-On Hop-Off Bus Tours
summary: Madrid City Tour and Madrid Tour official hop-on-off-off buses. €22/24h, 2 routes, audio in 14 languages.
content: Madrid has two hop-on hop-off tourist buses. The official Madrid City Tour (red buses) has 2 routes: Historical Madrid (17 stops, includes Sol, Royal Palace, Prado) and Modern Madrid (10 stops, includes Castellana, AZCA, Las Ventas). One ticket covers both routes. €22 for 24 hours, €26 for 48 hours. Audio guide in 14 languages including English. Buses run every 10-15 minutes in summer, every 20-25 in winter. The competitor Madrid Tour (yellow buses) is similar in price and route. Tip: in summer, the open-top buses get very hot — sit on the lower deck or wait until evening. For the best value, combine the bus with a Madrid Card (which includes the bus + free entry to 50+ museums). Buy tickets on the bus or online (slight discount).

title: Walking Tours (Free & Paid)
summary: Free tip-based walking tours (€5-10 tip) and specialty tours (tapas, flamenco, Jewish Madrid) from €20.
content: Madrid has a thriving walking tour scene. The free (tip-based) tours run twice daily (11am and 4pm) from Puerta del Sol — look for guides with red umbrellas (the main companies are Madrid Free Tours, OgoTours, and Cool Tours). Tour lasts 2.5-3 hours and covers Sol, Plaza Mayor, Royal Palace, Mercado de San Miguel, and the Habsburg old town. Tip €5-10 per person. Specialty tours cost €20-40: tapas tours (4-5 bars, food included, €35-50), flamenco tours (show + dinner, €50-70), Jewish Madrid history (€25), Civil War Madrid (€25), ghost tours (€20, evening). For an English-speaking guide for a private tour, expect €100-150 for 3 hours. Madrid has several specialized tour operators: Madrid Food Tour, Devour Tours, and Madrid Urban Adventures all have good reputations.

title: Segway Tours
summary: Segway tours of Madrid from €35 for 90 min. Popular for Retiro Park and the historic center. Age 12+.
content: Segway tours are a fun way to cover more ground than walking, especially for Retiro Park (which is large). Tours start at €35 for 90 minutes (Retiro Park) up to €65 for 3 hours (full city). Most tours include a 10-minute training session at the start. Weight limit: 45-117 kg. Age limit: usually 12+ (children under 16 must be accompanied by an adult). Helmet provided. The main operators are Madrid Segway Tour, Segway Trip Madrid, and Roll Madrid. All have English-speaking guides. Tours run year-round, with more frequent departures in spring and fall (summer afternoons are too hot for Segway). Tip: book the first tour of the day (9am) for fewer crowds and cooler weather. Retiro Park in the morning is particularly beautiful — the Segway lets you see all of it in 90 minutes.

title: Food & Tapas Tours
summary: Guided tapas crawls in La Latina, Malasaña, and San Miguel. €35-65, includes 4-5 stops with food and drinks.
content: Food tours are one of the best ways to experience Madrid's tapas culture, especially if you don't speak Spanish (the guide translates and helps you order). Tours run €35-65 per person for 3-4 hours, visiting 4-5 bars with 10+ tastings and 3-4 drinks (beer, wine, vermouth). Main operators: Madrid Food Tour (high-end, €65, 4 stops), Devour Madrid (mid-range, €55, 5 stops), and Tapas Taverns Tour (budget, €35, 4 stops). Most popular tours are in La Latina (traditional tapas), Malasaña (modern and creative), and around San Miguel market (tourist-friendly). Vegetarian options available on most tours — book ahead. For wine lovers, specialized sherry and Rioja tours are available (€50-70). Tip: don't eat lunch before a tapas tour — you'll be full by the end.

title: Bicycle Tours
summary: Bike tours of Madrid center and Retiro Park. €25-35 for 3 hours. Electric bikes (e-bikes) available for €40-50.
content: Madrid is mostly flat in the center, making it ideal for bike tours. Tours run €25-35 for 3 hours on a regular bike, €40-50 for an electric bike (recommended in summer when it's hot). Most tours start near Plaza de España or Sol and cover the historic center, Retiro Park, and Madrid Río. The main operators are Bravo Bike (oldest, English-speaking guides), Madrid Bike Tours, and Trixi. All include bike rental and helmet (optional). Weight limit: 120 kg. Children's seats and tag-along bikes available. Best time: spring and fall (10am or 4pm departures). Summer: book the 9am departure to avoid the heat. Madrid Río park has dedicated bike lanes and is especially bike-friendly.

title: Day Tours from Madrid (Toledo, Segovia, El Escorial)
summary: Organized day trips to Toledo, Segovia, El Escorial, Ávila. €50-100 with transport + guide.
content: If you don't want to organize a day trip yourself, several companies offer guided day tours from Madrid. The most popular destinations are Toledo (medieval capital, €50-70), Segovia (Roman aqueduct, €60-80), El Escorial (royal monastery, €50-70), and Ávila (medieval walls, €70-90). Tours include luxury bus transport, English-speaking guide, and sometimes museum tickets and lunch. Duration: 8-11 hours (full day). Main operators: Viator (largest selection), GetYourGuide (good for last-minute), Madrid Day Tours, and local specialists like Naturanda and Walking Tours Madrid. For a budget option, take the public bus or train yourself (see our Transport records) and explore independently — you'll save €30-50 and have more flexibility. Group tours have 15-50 people; private tours cost €200-400 but you set the pace.

title: Parque de Atracciones (Theme Park)
summary: Madrid's main amusement park in Casa de Campo. Roller coasters, water rides, family attractions. €35 adult, €28 child.
content: Parque de Atracciones de Madrid is the city's main amusement park, located in the Casa de Campo park (Metro: Batán, L10). It opened in 1969 and has 4 themed zones: Maquinismo (machinery/thrill rides), Gran Avenida (family rides), Naturaleza (nature-themed), and Nickelodeon Land (for younger kids). Top roller coasters: Abismo (2008, vertical drop), Tarántula (spinning coaster), and Tornado (2005, suspended). The park has 40+ rides total. Admission: €35 adult, €28 child (3-7), under 3 free. Discounts for booking online 7+ days ahead (€25 adult). Open daily June-August, weekends only rest of year. Fast Pass €15 extra (skip-the-line on 8 popular rides). Bring water and sunscreen — little shade in summer. Food inside is overpriced (€12-18 for a meal). Tip: arrive at opening (11am) and hit the big coasters first while lines are short.

title: Aquópolis Water Parks
summary: Two water parks in Madrid area (Villanueva and San Fernando). Wave pools, water slides, kids' areas. €28 adult, €19 child.
content: Aquópolis is the largest water park chain in Spain, with 2 locations in the Madrid area: Aquópolis Villanueva de la Cañada (west of Madrid, 30 min by car) and Aquópolis San Fernando de Henares (east, 25 min). Both have wave pools, water slides (some up to 20m high), lazy rivers, kids' areas, and sunbathing zones. Highlights: the Black Hole (enclosed slide), Zig Zag (multiple drop slides), and the wave pool. Admission: €28 adult, €19 child (under 10), under 3 free. Online discount: €22 adult if booked 7+ days ahead. Open mid-May to mid-September only. Getting there without a car: take Metro L12 to Puerta del Sur, then bus 529 to Villanueva; or Metro L7 to Henares, then bus 251 to San Fernando. Bring your own food (food inside is overpriced) and a waterproof phone pouch.

title: Zoo Aquarium de Madrid
summary: Madrid's main zoo in Casa de Campo. 6,000+ animals, dolphin show, aquarium, panda bears. €26 adult, €20 child.
content: Zoo Aquarium de Madrid is in Casa de Campo (Metro: Casa de Campo L5 or Batán L10 + 15 min walk). It's one of the oldest zoos in Europe (founded 1770) and houses 6,000+ animals across 130 species. Highlights: giant pandas (one of only 4 zoos in Europe with them), koalas, gorillas, tigers, and the aquarium (sharks, penguins, jellyfish). The dolphin show (extra €3) runs 2-3 times daily. New in 2023: the Asia zone with red pandas and snow leopards. Admission: €26 adult, €20 child (3-7), under 3 free. Online discount: €22 adult. Open daily 11am-7pm (extended in summer). Plan 3-4 hours for a full visit. Tip: bring a picnic (food inside is overpriced) and rent a stroller at the entrance if you have small kids (€6, the zoo is large).

title: IMAX & Planetarium (Pablo VI Cultural Center)
